metaTitle: Child Custody Under Muslim Law Bangladesh – Full Guide
title: Child Custody Under Muslim Law in Bangladesh: Mother's Rights, Father's Rights, and Court Process
metaDescription: Detailed guide to child custody under Muslim law in Bangladesh — hizanat rules, mother's rights, father's guardianship, court process, and recent Supreme Court decisions.
heroIntro: In Bangladesh, where the majority of the population is Muslim, child custody disputes are primarily governed by Islamic personal law as applied and developed by Bangladeshi courts. The principles of hizanat (maternal custody) and wilayat (paternal guardianship) form the backbone of the legal framework, and understanding their interplay — alongside the courts' overriding welfare principle — is essential for any parent involved in a custody dispute. This guide provides an in-depth analysis of child custody under Muslim law in Bangladesh.

quickAnswer.heading: ⚡ Quick Answer: Child Custody Under Muslim Law Bangladesh
points: Mothers hold the right of hizanat (physical custody) for sons up to 7 years and daughters until puberty.
Fathers hold wilayat (legal guardianship) over the child's person and property throughout minority.
The mother's hizanat can be forfeited by remarriage to a stranger or proven unfitness.
Courts apply the 'welfare of the child' as the paramount principle, overriding strict personal law rules when needed.
Both parents can approach the Family Court to enforce or challenge custody arrangements.

toc: Hizanat: The Islamic Law of Maternal Custody
When Does the Mother's Right of Hizanat End?
Role of Other Relatives in Custody (Agnates and Cognates)
How Courts Balance Islamic Law with the Welfare Principle
Filing a Custody Case Under Muslim Law in Bangladesh
Visitation Rights for the Non-Custodial Parent
Key Supreme Court Decisions on Muslim Law Custody Bangladesh

sections.h2: Hizanat: The Islamic Law of Maternal Custody
sections.content:
Hizanat is the Arabic term for the right and duty of a mother (or female relative) to have physical custody of a young child for the purposes of nurturing, care, and upbringing. Under Islamic jurisprudence as applied in Bangladesh, this right belongs first and foremost to the natural mother.

The rationale for maternal custody of young children is rooted in the child's need for maternal care during the formative years. Classical Islamic scholars — both Hanafi (followed by Sunni Muslims in Bangladesh) and Shafi'i — recognised that young children, especially infants and toddlers, require the physical and emotional care of a mother in ways that fathers are typically unable to replicate.

Under the Hanafi school (the predominant school in Bangladesh), the age limits for hizanat are:

  • For boys: Until the age of 7 years, the mother has the primary right of custody.
  • For girls: Until the onset of puberty (which courts generally treat as approximately 9–15 years depending on individual development), the mother has the primary right of custody.

After these ages, the father (as legal guardian) becomes entitled to custody — though as discussed below, Bangladesh courts apply the welfare principle flexibly rather than rigidly adhering to these age limits.

If the biological mother is unavailable or unfit, hizanat passes to other female relatives in the following order of priority: maternal grandmother, paternal grandmother, full sisters, uterine sisters (daughters of the same mother but different father), consanguine sisters (same father, different mother), maternal aunts, and finally paternal aunts. Courts follow this order of preference while always keeping the child's welfare central.

sections.h2: When Does the Mother's Right of Hizanat End?
sections.content:
The mother's right of hizanat can be forfeited before the age limit in specific circumstances recognised under both Islamic law and Bangladesh court practice:

  1. Remarriage to a Stranger: If the mother remarries a person who is not a mahram of the child (i.e., not a relative within the forbidden degrees of marriage to the child), she automatically forfeits her right of hizanat under classical Hanafi law. Courts in Bangladesh apply this rule but also consider whether the new stepfather has a good relationship with the child.
  2. Gross Immorality or Misconduct: If the mother is found guilty of immoral conduct — such as living in an adulterous relationship, chronic substance abuse, or prostitution — the court will terminate her custody rights.
  3. Neglect or Ill-Treatment: Proven neglect of the child's basic needs — food, clothing, healthcare, education — can lead to forfeiture.
  4. Apostasy: Under classical Islamic law, a mother who leaves Islam loses her right of hizanat over Muslim children, though this ground has rarely been applied by Bangladesh courts in modern practice.
  5. Separation by Distance: If the mother relocates to a place so far away that the father would be deprived of his right to see and supervise the child, the court may transfer custody to the father.
  6. General Unfitness: Any circumstance that renders the mother unsuitable to raise the child — including serious mental or physical illness — can be grounds for forfeiture.

Importantly, Bangladesh courts have repeatedly held that poverty alone is not a ground for depriving a mother of hizanat. The father's maintenance obligation continues even when the mother has custody, and financial inability of the mother does not per se make her unfit.

While the mother has hizanat (physical custody), the father holds wilayat — the legal guardianship and authority over the child's person and property. These rights exist independently of the custody arrangement and continue throughout the child's minority (until age 18 under Bangladesh law).

The father's rights as wali (guardian) include:

  • Religious upbringing: The father determines the child's religion. Children of a Muslim father must be raised as Muslims.
  • Education decisions: School selection, subject choices, and educational planning fall within the father's guardianship.
  • Property management: The father manages any property owned by or inherited by the minor child until the child reaches majority.
  • Travel and documentation: Passports, visas, and travel consent for the child require the father's approval as guardian.
  • Medical decisions: Major medical decisions about the child's health fall within the father's purview, though in practice the custodial mother handles day-to-day healthcare.
  • Marriage guardianship (ijbar): For daughters, the father (or grandfather) acts as wali nikah (marriage guardian) in arranging marriage.

Even when the mother has physical custody, the father's role as legal guardian remains intact. Courts frequently make orders specifying how guardianship duties are to be exercised so that both parents can fulfil their respective roles. If you have questions about your guardianship rights, a family lawyer in Bangladesh can advise you.

sections.h2: Role of Other Relatives in Custody (Agnates and Cognates)
sections.content:
When neither the mother nor the father is able or willing to assume custody, Islamic law provides a detailed scheme of alternative custodians from among the child's relatives. Courts in Bangladesh have occasionally needed to apply these rules in cases of parental death, incapacity, or disappearance.

The priority order among female relatives (for the right of hizanat) after the biological mother is:

  1. Maternal grandmother (nani), ascending in order;
  2. Paternal grandmother (dadi), ascending in order;
  3. Full sisters;
  4. Uterine (half) sisters (same mother, different father);
  5. Consanguine (half) sisters (same father, different mother);
  6. Full maternal aunts;
  7. Paternal aunts.

Among male relatives (for guardianship/wilayat when the father is unavailable), the order of priority is:

  1. Paternal grandfather;
  2. Full brother;
  3. Half-brother (consanguine);
  4. Full paternal uncle;
  5. Half paternal uncle.

Courts have the power under the Guardians and Wards Act 1890 to appoint any suitable person as guardian — whether a relative or not — if it is for the welfare of the child. The personal law priority list is a guide, not an absolute rule.

sections.h2: How Courts Balance Islamic Law with the Welfare Principle
sections.content:
The single most important principle in Bangladesh's approach to child custody is the welfare of the child. While Islamic personal law provides the framework, courts have consistently held that the child's welfare is the paramount — and overriding — consideration.

This means that:

  • If giving custody to the father (even after the hizanat age expires) would better serve the child's welfare, the mother may retain custody beyond the prescribed age limits.
  • If the mother is unfit but the next-in-line Islamic relative is also unfit, the court can appoint an entirely different guardian who is not in the Islamic list of priorities.
  • Courts can impose conditions on custody — such as requiring the custodial parent to live in a particular city, maintain the child's existing school enrollment, or facilitate regular contact with the other parent.
  • The welfare principle allows courts to adapt the personal law rules to 21st-century family structures, including cases involving joint parenting, working mothers, and fathers who are the primary caregivers.

The Appellate Division of the Supreme Court of Bangladesh has in multiple decisions emphasised that the welfare of the child supersedes the technical rules of personal law when the two conflict. This progressive judicial approach provides both parents with a fair opportunity to argue their case on its merits.

sections.h2: Filing a Custody Case Under Muslim Law in Bangladesh
sections.content:
Whether you are a mother seeking to retain or enforce your hizanat rights, or a father seeking custody or enhanced visitation, the procedural steps are the same:

  1. Consult a Specialist Lawyer: Engage a family law specialist who is familiar with both Islamic personal law and the Family Courts Ordinance 1985.
  2. File at the Family Court: The Family Court at the district level (Senior Assistant Judge's Court) has jurisdiction over custody and guardianship matters.
  3. Draft the Petition: The petition should cite the relevant provisions of the Guardians and Wards Act 1890 and the Family Courts Ordinance 1985, and set out the factual basis for the claim.
  4. Seek Interim Relief: If the child has been wrongfully withheld, apply for an interim custody order at the first hearing.
  5. Attend Hearings: Both parents must attend hearings and present evidence — character witnesses, school records, home visit reports, and any relevant documents.
  6. Final Order: The court passes a decree on custody, visitation schedule, and maintenance.
  7. Enforcement: If the other party refuses to comply with the order, file a contempt application before the same court.

sections.h2: Visitation Rights for the Non-Custodial Parent
sections.content:
Even when one parent is granted custody, the non-custodial parent retains the right to regular, meaningful contact with the child. Bangladesh courts recognise mulaqat (visitation) as an enforceable right under both Islamic law and general family law.

Courts typically specify the visitation schedule in the custody decree, including:

  • Regular weekly or bi-weekly visits (e.g., every weekend from Friday to Saturday);
  • Extended stays during school holidays and Eid vacations;
  • Special occasions such as birthdays and religious festivals;
  • Telephone and video call access if the parents live in different cities.

Denial of court-ordered visitation is treated as contempt of court and can result in fine or imprisonment of the defaulting parent. Courts may also vary the custody arrangement in favour of the aggrieved parent if visitation is persistently denied.

If you are being denied contact with your child, immediately consult a family lawyer to file an enforcement or contempt application before the Family Court.

sections.h2: Key Supreme Court Decisions on Muslim Law Custody Bangladesh
sections.content:
Several landmark decisions from the Supreme Court of Bangladesh have shaped the modern application of Muslim law custody rules. These include:

  • Welfare as the Paramount Test: The Appellate Division has repeatedly held that in all custody matters, the welfare of the child is the paramount and overriding consideration, which can override even clear rules of Muslim personal law (see various cases under the Guardians and Wards Act).
  • Mother's Right Beyond Age Limits: High Court Division benches have upheld the mother's right to retain custody beyond the technical hizanat age when the child's welfare so requires, particularly where the child has a strong emotional bond with the mother and the father is shown to be unfit.
  • Poverty Is No Ground: Courts have consistently held that the mother's poverty alone cannot deprive her of hizanat — the father's maintenance obligation must be enforced instead.
  • Habeas Corpus for Child Recovery: The High Court Division has used its habeas corpus jurisdiction under Article 102 of the Constitution to order the return of children wrongfully withheld by one parent in defiance of a custody order.

For advice on how these principles apply to your specific situation, contact a qualified Supreme Court lawyer in Bangladesh who can provide guidance based on current judicial trends.

faqs.question: Does a Muslim father automatically get custody when his son turns 7 in Bangladesh?
faqs.answer: Not automatically. While Islamic law provides that the mother's hizanat for sons ends at age 7, Bangladesh courts apply the welfare of the child as the paramount principle. If the child's welfare is better served by remaining with the mother, the court can allow her to retain custody beyond age 7. The father must apply to court and demonstrate that custody should be transferred.

faqs.question: Can a Muslim mother remarry and still keep custody of her children?
faqs.answer: Under classical Hanafi law, remarriage to a stranger (someone not a close relative of the child) results in forfeiture of hizanat. However, Bangladesh courts have softened this rule by also examining the child's welfare — if the new stepfather treats the child well and the child is thriving, some courts have been reluctant to transfer custody automatically. Each case is assessed on its individual facts.

faqs.question: Can grandparents apply for custody of grandchildren in Bangladesh?
faqs.answer: Yes. Under the Guardians and Wards Act 1890, any person with a sufficient interest in the child's welfare — including grandparents — can apply to be appointed as guardian. Grandparents are also recognised within the Islamic priority list for hizanat when the parents are unavailable or unfit.

faqs.question: What maintenance is the father obliged to pay during the mother's custody period?
faqs.answer: Under Muslim law, the father is obliged to pay maintenance (nafaqa) for the children regardless of which parent has custody. This includes food, clothing, shelter, healthcare, and education costs. The mother is also entitled to a fee (ajr al-hadana) for her services as custodian if she demands it. Courts fix the maintenance amount based on the father's income and the child's needs.

faqs.question: What happens if the custodial parent takes the child abroad without permission?
faqs.answer: Taking a child abroad without the other parent's consent or a court order can amount to contempt of court and child abduction. The aggrieved parent should immediately apply to the High Court for a habeas corpus order and seek a port alert to prevent the child's removal from Bangladesh. Consult an experienced family lawyer immediately in such situations.
